retrieved_docs.title: Regular Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin, a natural endogenous hormone synthesized and secreted by pancreatic beta cells, has a structure of 51 amino acids.

retrieved_docs.title: Human Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin’s significant actions focus on storing excess energy in a fed state. Insulin promotes glycogen synthesis, lipid synthesis, protein synthesis, DNA synthesis, and cellular growth and differentiation. Once glucose gets absorbed from a meal, it enters the blood, and then the pancreas releases insulin. Insulin synthesis occurs in the beta cells of the pancreas initially as preproinsulin. Preproinsulin then converts to proinsulin, which then transforms into a single peptide with A, B, and C peptide units. The A and B peptides are joined by disulfide bonds to make insulin and are secreted into the bloodstream. Insulin binds to its cellular receptor. The insulin receptor is composed of alpha subunits, beta subunits, and a tyrosine kinase enzyme. When insulin binds to the alpha subunit, this triggers phosphorylation and activation of the target proteins intracellularly by the tyrosine kinase leading to many effects on cellular metabolism. Activation of the insulin receptor also leads to increased expression of GLUT (a glucose transporter) to the membrane surface and promotes the entry of glucose to the intracellular compartment and then undergoes cellular metabolism. Insulin signals glucose conversion to glycogen for storage and the formation of acetyl coenzyme A and triacylglycerol, which get stored in adipose tissue. Also, insulin directs amino acids for protein synthesis. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Insulin Metabolic Effects -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin is a peptide hormone comprised of 51 amino acids distributed among 2 peptide chains, the A and B chains, of 21 and 30 amino acid residues, respectively. Disulfide bonds of cysteine residues connect the 2 chains. Preproinsulin is the original precursor protein of insulin. It is a single-chain polypeptide consisting of proinsulin and signal peptide sequences. After translating into the endoplasmic reticulum, preproinsulin cleaves at its signal peptide, releasing proinsulin. Proinsulin is a single chain containing insulin’s A and B chains continuously, joined through a segment known as the C domain. Dibasic residues flank the C domain at each end. At the site of each dibasic residue, a trypsin-like enzyme cleaves proinsulin. This cleavage finally releases insulin along with a C-peptide. Until metabolically needed, insulin is stored within glucose-regulated secretory vesicles as zinc insulin hexamers. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Peptide -- Molecular Level -- Peptide Hormones
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin is a 51 amino acid peptide hormone that consists of two disulfide-linked peptide chains. [12] IGF-1 (insulin-like-growth-factor- 1) family are also peptide hormones but have three disulfide bonds. [12] Insulin's role in the body is multifaceted to control metabolic homeostasis, including glucose uptake from the blood and storage of glucose as glycogen in the liver.

retrieved_docs.title: Regular Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In general, insulin stimulates protein synthesis by increasing the number of translational efficiency ribosomes in skeletal muscle and liver and accelerating the transport of amino acids into hepatocytes, fibroblasts, and skeletal muscle, which in turn, causes a ribosome-catalyzed reaction and translation of the genetic code in specific messenger RNAs. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hormones -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The peptide hormones are water soluble molecules composed of amino acids (AA) linked by amide bonds. They exist as single polypeptide chains or as multimeric proteins, ranging in size from 3 to 200 AAs. They commonly derive from a single gene, with multimeric proteins being an exception. The peptide hormones act on cell surface receptors and therefore must be capable of exocytosis after translation. First, a ribosome translates a signal sequence that docks it to a signal recognition particle (SRP) on the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER). Translation continues within the RER, and the growing peptide is cleaved from its signal sequence, forming a large preprohormone, which is cleaved to a prohormone, packaged in a vesicle, and sent to the Golgi apparatus. In the Golgi, peptides are cleaved into their final form and packaged into secretory vesicles that enter the cytoplasm. These vesicles undergo exocytosis upon cellular stimulus. Classic examples of this processing are pre-pro-PTH, pro-PTH and PTH as well as pre-pro-insulin, proinsulin, and insulin. The secretory vesicles often contain cleavage products in addition to the hormone as seen with insulin and C-peptide, which are co-secreted in equimolar concentrations. In addition to cleavages, other modifications occur in the RER and Golgi, namely N-linked and O-linked glycosylation of AA side chains. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Regular Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin is considered the most potent anabolic hormone known until today, and its effects on the body are necessary for tissue development, growth, and maintenance of glucose homeostasis. Insulin action starts by binding to two cell receptors, which are alpha and beta, that are linked by two disulfide bonds into a complex that is a heterotetrameric membrane. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastric Inhibitory Peptide -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP), formerly termed "gastric inhibitory peptide," was first isolated in 1973 from porcine small intestine for its capacity to suppress gastric hydrochloric acid secretion. Comparative studies by John Brown and Raymond Pederson in 1970 provided the first evidence for its existence. [1] Subsequent investigations in 1980 demonstrated that GIP is only a weak inhibitor of acid secretion but a potent stimulator of postprandial insulin release. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Human Insulin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the action of insulin on glucose metabolism. Review the synthesis of insulin in pancreatic beta cells. Outline the relationship between insulin and potassium and its importance in the management of patients with diabetic ketoacidosis. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication about the use of insulin to enhance outcomes for patients affected by diabetes mellitus. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin Resistance -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: An alternative classification of insulin resistance exists and is based on the site of dysfunction with respect to the insulin receptor. This classification system includes pre-receptor, receptor, and post-receptor etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin acts by directly binding to its receptors on the plasma membranes of the cells. These receptors are present on all the cells, but their density depends on the type of cells, with the maximum density being on the hepatic cells and adipocytes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon is a peptide hormone that is composed of amino acids, placing it within the broader category of amino acids, peptides, or proteins; therefore, glucagon is classified as a member of this protein‑based group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2394
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human insulin analogue, is classified as a member of the group “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting,” indicating that it is employed in combination regimens with longer‑acting insulins for diabetes management. Mechanistically, insulin aspart exerts its glucose‑lowering effect by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein), the primary receptor mediating insulin’s metabolic actions. This dual characterization highlights both its therapeutic role within mixed‑insulin therapy and its specific molecular interaction with the INSR protein, informing its use in achieving optimal glycemic control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropoietin and glucagon are both classified as members of the broader category “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein,” indicating that each is a peptide‑based biomolecule. As a peptide hormone, glucagon can be managed therapeutically by an approach described as “رد فعل تأقي,” suggesting that this specific type of counter‑reaction or antagonistic intervention may be used in the treatment of conditions involving glucagon.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin and beef are both recognized as proteinaceous entities, belonging to the broader category of proteiny substances, which highlights their shared characteristic of being composed of protein.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1903
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) analogues are classified as members of the exenatid family, indicating that they share the structural and pharmacologic characteristics of exenatid compounds, which function as GLP‑1 receptor agonists commonly used in the treatment of type 2 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 934
retrieved_docs.content: The INSR protein, human functions as the molecular target for insulin aspart, human, a rapid‑acting insulin analog used in diabetes management. Binding of insulin aspart, human to the INSR protein, human initiates the insulin signaling cascade that promotes glucose uptake and regulates blood‑sugar levels, making this interaction central to the drug’s therapeutic effect. Recognizing that the INSR protein, human is the target of insulin aspart, human informs the understanding of its mechanism of action, guides dosing strategies, and supports optimized patient care in the treatment of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2450
retrieved_docs.content: INSR protein, human serves as the molecular target of insulin aspart, human, a rapid‑acting insulin analog. By binding to the INSR protein, insulin aspart, human facilitates glucose uptake and lowers blood glucose levels, making it an appropriate therapeutic option for managing both مرض السكري نوع 1 and مرض السكري نوع 2. Consequently, the interaction between insulin aspart, human and its target INSR protein underlies its use in the treatment of these two major forms of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1679
retrieved_docs.content: L‑karnitin and metreleptin are both classified as members of the broader category “amino acids and derivatives,” indicating that each compound is biochemically derived from amino‑acid structures. This grouping reflects their shared origin in amino‑acid metabolism, even though their physiological roles differ: L‑karnitin functions primarily in the transport of long‑chain fatty acids into mitochondria for β‑oxidation, while metreleptin is a recombinant analog of the hormone leptin used therapeutically to replace deficient leptin activity. Recognizing both agents as amino‑acid‑derived molecules highlights the diverse functional spectrum of this chemical class in normal metabolism and in clinical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: that can disrupt cellular membranes and provoke immune reactions, while human insulin is the principal hormone governing carbohydrate metabolism and energy storage. Toxoids, which are inactivated toxins, are used to safely stimulate protective immune physiology without causing disease. Together, these diverse organisms, molecules, anatomical structures, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the wide range of factors that influence human physiological processes and underscore their importance in clinical assessment, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 314
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives comprise several biologically important compounds, including L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, and glutamine, each of which is classified as a member of this chemical group. These molecules play key roles in cellular metabolism, energy production, methyl‑group transfer, and nitrogen balance, making them essential for normal physiological function and for the management of various metabolic disorders. In contrast, metreleptin, a recombinant leptin analog used to treat leptin deficiency and related metabolic conditions, does not belong to the amino acids and derivatives category; it is a protein‑based therapeutic rather than an amino‑acid‑derived compound. Recognizing the correct classification of these entities supports accurate understanding of their mechanisms of action and appropriate clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: and therapeutically. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications link specific compounds to their mechanisms, chemical families, and clinical applications, providing a structured framework for endocrine evaluation, diagnostic testing, and comprehensive management of diabetes and related metabolic disorders, with biguanides explicitly identified as a subclass of non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering agents used to reduce blood glucose in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2468
retrieved_docs.content: The human INSR protein (insulin receptor) serves as the molecular target of insulin aspart, a rapid‑acting insulin analog. Insulin aspart, in turn, is employed in the therapeutic management of مرض السكري نوع 2 (type 2 diabetes mellitus). By binding to the INSR protein, insulin aspart facilitates cellular glucose uptake and helps control hyperglycemia in patients with type 2 diabetes, highlighting the direct link between the drug’s target, its pharmacologic action, and the disease it is used to treat.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2066
retrieved_docs.content: Insulins and analogues are a specific class of medications that belong to the broader category of drugs used in diabetes, representing a subclass within the overall group of diabetes therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1353
retrieved_docs.content: Insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes that are intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting formulations include insulin aspart, which is classified as a human insulin analog and therefore is a member of this therapeutic group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon (ACTH) and glukagon are both peptide hormones that belong to the broader category of amino acids, peptides, or proteins. Because they are members of this biochemical class, a deficiency (“nedostatek”) can be appropriately applied as a qualifier to describe clinical states of insufficient ACTH or glukagon. This reflects that both hormones share structural characteristics as protein‑based signaling molecules and that their lack can be recognized and described in medical contexts.
